heading: PROEMIO 13
card_summary: This chunk of the Proem continues Zorzi's argument for a universal harmony linking the sensible and intelligible worlds, grounded in the Kabbalistic vision of Ezekiel's chariot (the "wheel within a wheel," haophan betoc haophan) and the Johannine Logos theology. Zorzi moves through Moses, Ezekiel, and John as witnesses to this cosmic harmony, then launches an extended defense of sacred music as a vehicle of that harmony, drawing on Pythagoras, Boethius, Cicero, Terpander, Timotheus, and the biblical examples of David's curing of Saul. The chunk closes with Zorzi's programmatic statement about the three "canticles" of the De Harmonia Mundi — each a diapason of eight tones — covering respectively the mutual consonance of the two worlds with the Archetype, the consonance of all things with Christ the Messiah, and the consonance of man as the bond of all things. He also defends his use of original Hebrew divine names, citing Origen's dictum that sacred names lose their force in translation.
essay:
## Overview

Chunk 41 (pp. 181–185) sits at the climax of the long Proem and performs two intertwined tasks: (1) it consolidates the visionary warrant for the work — reading Ezekiel's chariot, Moses at Sinai, and the Johannine Prologue as converging testimonies to a hidden cosmic harmony — and (2) it announces the architectonic plan of the three *cantica* that will organise the entire *De Harmonia Mundi*. The theological key throughout is the Kabbalistic-Neoplatonic identification of the *Verbum* / Logos with the intelligible world that contains and sustains the sensible.

## The Wheel within a Wheel: Ezekiel's Chariot

The chunk opens mid-sentence in Ezekiel's visionary description of the *Merkavah* (chariot throne). Zorzi quotes the Hebrew:

> *haophan betoc haophan*, cioè una ruota in mezzo a un'altra ruota

<cite>p. 181</cite>

This phrase — Ezekiel 1:16, the *ophanim* or "wheel within a wheel" — is read cosmologically: the mundane world of rotary, spherical form is situated *in the midst of* the intelligible sphere, which is itself identified with the *Verbum*. The Johannine gloss ("ciò che è stato fatto, in lui era la vita") is cited immediately (John 1:3–4): creation exists *in* the Word, which is the centre of the supramundane circle. This braiding of the Hebrew prophetic vision (Ezekiel) with Johannine Logos theology is characteristic of Zorzi's Christian Kabbalist method: the *Merkavah* literature and the Fourth Gospel are read as mutually illuminating.

## Moses, the Veil, and Esoteric Hermeneutics

Zorzi then reads the "twin peaks" typologically. John sees Christ on the "two-peaked Parnassus" (the double nature, divine and human, in Christ's breast); Moses contemplates the same mystery on the "twin peaks of Sinai." The image of the veil (*velame*) that Moses used with the Israelites is developed at length as the paradigm for all esoteric writing:

> consegnò al popolo misteri profondissimi sotto il velame di una narrazione storica, riservando uno spazio a beneficio dei sapienti perché potessero accedere, scostato il velo e svelato il volto, fino ai recessi della sapienza più profonda

<cite>p. 182</cite>

The fourfold hermeneutic — *significati morali, allegorici e anagogici* (the Dantesque three-plus-literal scheme) — is invoked as the ladder of ascent from inferior, sensible realities to invisible truths, guided by "numeri affini e proporzioni armoniche." This passage establishes that harmonic number-theory is not merely mathematical but the very vehicle of anagogical scriptural interpretation.

## The Power of Sacred Music: Classical and Biblical Witnesses

The argument pivots to the therapeutic and ontological power of music, adduced as empirical proof of universal harmonic sympathy. The chain of authorities is dense:

- **Pythagoras** — calmed a young man's madness by changing melody (Cicero and Boethius cited)
- **Terpander, Arion, and Ismenias of Thebes** — healed the sick of Lesbos and Ionia
- **Timotheus** — so inflamed Alexander the Macedonian (per Dion) that he rushed to arms "like a madman"
- **David's lyre** — exorcised the evil spirit from Saul, because the demon "non poté sopportare un autentico accordo, ovvero ciò che gli è massimamente avverso"

The passage moves fluidly between Italian and Latin (the Latin section, pp. 183–184, is given in the original):

> Ad sacra quoque conscendentis Saulis regis furor sic Davidis cithara temperabatur, ut ad pristinam reduceretur quietem fugato malo daemone, qui ab harmonia illa collapsus concentum aliquem verum, veluti ipsi inimicum, sustinere nequit.

<cite>p. 183</cite>

The theological inference is precise: the evil demon is the ontological contrary of harmony; true *concentus* is literally intolerable to it. This is not merely therapeutic metaphor but a statement about the metaphysical structure of evil as dissonance.

## Sacred Music as Vehicle of Cosmic Consonance

Zorzi then argues that it was David himself — the *regius propheta* and *restaurator* of the sacred cult — who introduced harmonious song into the liturgy precisely because he perceived the "mysteriosi vincoli tra Dio e la natura." The Davidic Psalms are held up as the supreme instance of this principle:

> In essi, infatti, si cantano, come in una sorta di accompagnamento musicale di tutta la legge, gli armonici accordi tra entrambi i mondi e l'Archetipo, e la consonanza restituita dal Messia a tutte le cose

<cite>p. 184</cite>

This sentence crystallises the three-tier cosmology of *De Harmonia Mundi*: the two worlds (sensible/intelligible), the Archetype (God as the supreme *opifex*), and the Messiah as the agent of restitution. The Psalms are not merely devotional poetry but an encoded map of the entire harmonic order.

## The Prophets as Masters of All Learning

Zorzi extends the claim: all the biblical prophets are shown to possess geometry (Moses, Ezekiel, John), numerical proportion (Moses, Ezekiel, Daniel, John), and supreme music (the Psalms, the Song of Songs). This music surpasses even Orpheus and Amphion. The pagan philosophers and lawmakers — Hermes Trismegistus, Aglaophemus, Orpheus, Hesiod, Pythagoras, Archytas, Plato, Parmenides, Charondas, Zamolxis, Lycurgus — are acknowledged as recipients of wisdom but are to be admitted only insofar as they "concordes videbuntur" with the harmony Zorzi proposes; where they dissonant, they are to be set aside "non aliter, quam inutiles chordas."

> Verumenimvero Pater Beatissime, quod a plerisque memoriae proditum est, non aliunde Mercurium, Aglaophemum, Orpheum, Hesiodum, Pythagoram, Architam, Platonem... sumpsisse, quod traderent.

<cite>p. 184</cite>

This is the *prisca theologia* topos standard in Ficinian and Zorzi's milieu: the pagan sages ultimately derive from Hebrew wisdom. But Zorzi sharpens it into a methodological criterion: consonance with Scripture is the test of admissibility.

## The Three Canticles: The Architectonic Plan

The chunk closes with the definitive structural statement of the whole work. The *De Harmonia Mundi* will be divided into three *cantica*, each comprising eight tones (an octave, *diapason*):

1. **First canticle**: the mutual consonance of the two worlds (sensible and intelligible) with each other and with the Archetype
2. **Second canticle**: the consonance of all things with the Messiah as head, and the *concentus* restored by Christ to all things broken by sin
3. **Third canticle**: man as the "vincolo consonantissimo" of all things — the most harmonious bond

> complecti posse iudicavimus, hinc quodlibet canticum octo tonis, diapason videlicet perfecto, consummabitur

<cite>p. 185</cite>

The musical architecture (three canticles × eight tones = the three diapasons) is thus the formal expression of the cosmological programme: the Trinity of relations (World–World, World–Christ, Man) mapped onto the perfect octave.

## Defense of Hebrew Divine Names

Finally, Zorzi invokes **Origen** to justify his use of original Hebrew (and where necessary, approximate Latin) names for God and the powers: Origen held that sacred names translated into another tongue "propriam vim minime retinere" (retain no proper force). This anticipates the work's extensive use of Hebrew Kabbalistic terminology and is an early statement of a position that would later be associated with theurgic Neoplatonism (Iamblichus on the untranslatability of divine names).

## Connections to the Larger Project

This passage functions as the true *prooemium* to the entire three-part structure. The cosmological triad — Archetype / two worlds / Messiah as restorer — is the spine of *De Harmonia Mundi*. The Kabbalistic reading of Ezekiel's chariot, the Davidic Psalms as harmonic cosmology, and the hierarchical hermeneutic (veil / unveiling) all recur throughout. The defence of Hebrew names signals that the work will operate as genuine Christian Kabbalah, not merely allegorical decoration.
